food_subgroup: Game meat dishes — stir-fried venison with vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried deer/venison with onion and mushroom. FOODON_00002473 (stir-fried meat dish) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers venison as a raw ingredient category. No specific code exists for the stir-fried preparation with onion and mushroom; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for stir-fried venison dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried deer meat with onion and mushroom is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of sliced venison, onion wedges, and mushrooms served over or alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Venison, raw (Deer meat, raw) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 17162; supplemented with USDA data for stir-fried preparation adjustments and vegetable components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for venison (deer meat, raw, NDB 17162 / FDC ID 174491) as the primary protein-fat-energy anchor. Cooking adjustments for stir-frying (moisture loss ~15%, fat addition from ~5 g oil per 100 g raw meat) and contributions from onion and mushroom (USDA FDC IDs 170000 and 169251) are incorporated proportionally. The composite dish is estimated at approximately 60% venison, 20% onion, 20% mushroom by cooked weight, with soy-based sauce contributing sodium. Values represent the cooked composite dish per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat-and-vegetable stir-fry dish with low-GI vegetables (onion GI ~10, mushroom GI ~10) and negligible starchy carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried venison with onion and mushroom. GI is estimated at 30 based on the low-GI profile of the dish components: venison contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrate (protein-fat dominant), onion has a GI of approximately 10 (Atkinson et al. 2021), and mushroom has a GI of approximately 10–15. The composite dish carbohydrate content is low (4.2 g/100 g) and predominantly from low-GI vegetables. GL per 300 g serving = (30 × 12.6 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.8. This is consistent with the low-GI, low-GL profile of non-starchy vegetable and lean meat stir-fries documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, which represents over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load from non-starchy vegetables and lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 30 and a GL of 3.8 per serving, this dish has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice in large quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean game meat with favourable fat profile and functional vegetable components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Venison is a lean red meat with lower saturated fat than beef or pork; the dish provides iron, zinc, and B12 with a relatively low saturated fat content of 1.6 g/100 g, making it a more cardiovascular-friendly red meat option, though sodium from the sauce warrants attention for those with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Deer Meat with Onion and Mushroom (洋葱蘑菇炒鹿肉) is a Chinese-style wok dish featuring thinly sliced venison (deer meat) rapidly stir-fried over high heat with onion wedges and mushrooms, typically seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and ginger. The dish is served at select Chinese seafood and game-meat restaurants in Singapore, such as Riverfront Seafood at Boat Quay, where it is priced at approximately SGD 22 per portion. Venison is a lean, dark-red game meat with a slightly gamey, rich flavour and a firm yet tender texture when properly wok-fried. The onion contributes sweetness and a slight crunch, while the mushrooms add umami depth and a soft, meaty texture. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-dense (approximately 18.5 g protein per 100 g) and relatively low in fat (5.8 g/100 g), with a favourable saturated fat profile compared to conventional red meats. The glycemic index is low (estimated GI ~30) due to the dominance of non-starchy vegetables and lean protein, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Iron content is notable at 2.8 mg/100 g, supporting haemoglobin synthesis. The primary clinical concern is sodium from the sauce base, which can reach 1260 mg per restaurant serving, relevant for hypertensive patients. The dish is not halal-certified in most Singapore contexts as deer meat sourcing and preparation may not meet halal standards. It is dairy-free and gluten content depends on the soy sauce used.
common_user_queries: Is stir-fried deer meat good for diabetics?
How much protein is in deer meat?
Where can I eat deer meat in Singapore?
Is deer meat halal in Singapore?
meal_context: shared dish in a multi-course Chinese meal

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for stir-fried deer meat with onion and mushroom exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy venison data with proportional adjustments for the composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed GI tables for the component ingredients. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Vegetables' (input) to 'Meat and alternatives' as the primary ingredient is deer meat (venison). 2. Cuisine tags in the input ('Seafood Dish', 'Crab', 'Lobster') appear to be restaurant-level tags for Riverfront Seafood and are not applicable to this specific dish; they have been excluded. 3. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; a direct laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo entry would be required to upgrade confidence to high. 4. Halal status is marked false as deer meat in Singapore is not routinely halal-certified; this should be verified per restaurant. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as venison is a non-ruminant game meat with negligible industrial trans fat and the cooking method does not introduce trans fats. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy venison entry and is not estimable from the composite dish components with confidence. 7. GI set to 30 as an analogous estimate per Atkinson et al. (2021) AJCN peer-reviewed GI tables; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). GL per 300 g serving = (30 × 12.6) / 100 = 3.8. 8. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and oyster sauce in the sauce base; this is the primary clinical concern for hypertensive patients.
